participants.extracted: Adult women of childbearing and childrearing age with clinically diagnosed rheumatoid arthritis who were under the care of a rheumatologist and were contemplating having children or more children. A total of 188 women enrolled; 144 women completed both pre- and post-intervention questionnaires and comprised the final analyzed sample (78 in the decision-aid group and 66 in the control group). The mean age was about 31 years, most had tertiary education, and many were married.

interventions.extracted: The intervention was a self-administered decision aid titled “Motherhood choices: decision Aid for women with rheumatoid arthritis.” It was developed using the Ottawa Decision Support Framework and International Patient Decision Aid Standards (IPDAS), with input from consumer and expert panels and pilot testing. The final resource was a 45-page electronic or paper guide that provided information on rheumatoid arthritis, conception, pregnancy, and parenting; explained the effects of RA and RA medications on fertility, pregnancy, breastfeeding, and postpartum issues; included women’s stories; and contained decision-making worksheets such as a decision tree, pros-and-cons scales, a support network tool, a knowledge checklist, and note taking. In the trial, women in the intervention group completed the baseline questionnaire and then received a link to the decision aid by email or SurveyMonkey and were asked to read it within two weeks, with follow-up after approximately two to four weeks.

outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure outcomes at baseline (pre-intervention) and follow-up (post-intervention, approximately 2 to 4 weeks after baseline) using identical questionnaires except for demographic items at follow-up. Primary outcomes were: (1) pregnancy, rheumatic arthritis, and parenting knowledge assessed with the Pregnancy in Rheumatoid Arthritis Questionnaire (PiRAQ), a 39-item scale scored 0 to 39, with higher scores indicating greater knowledge; and (2) decisional conflict assessed with the Decisional Conflict Scale (DCS), a 16-item self-report scale scored from 0 to 100, including subscales for informed, values clarity, support, uncertainty, and effective decision. Secondary outcomes were: arthritis self-efficacy assessed with the short-form Arthritis Self-Efficacy Scale (ASES), an 8-item scale scored 1 to 10; anxiety assessed with the Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale-Anxiety subscale (HADS-A), 7 items scored 0 to 21; depression assessed with the Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale-Depression subscale (HADS-D), 7 items scored 0 to 21; whether rheumatoid arthritis complicated the decision to have children (a yes/no/unsure item); and decisional certainty regarding having children in the future, rated on a scale from -5 (definitely would not have children) to +5 (definitely would have children), with 0 indicating unsure.
